sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Traumatic amputation refers to the partial or complete removal of a limb or part of a limb, typically resulting from severe trauma such as motor vehicle collisions, industrial accidents, agricultural incidents, or machinery injuries.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Visible loss or mutilation of limb segment
- Severe bleeding or haemorrhagic shock
- Pain, distress, agitation
- Soft tissue injury, exposed bone, possible contamination
- Entrapment of the limb
- Signs of hypovolaemia: tachycardia, pallor, hypotension
- Potential for traumatic shock (altered mental status, cold, clammy skin)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Time-critical priorities are catastrophic haemorrhage control, analgesia, and rapid transport
- Entrapment or prolonged crush force raises concern for crush syndrome and reperfusion injury on release
- Retrieve and preserve the amputated part whenever safe and practical
- Treat the amputated part as potentially reimplantable and avoid direct contact with ice
- Amputations are highly distressing; communicate clearly and compassionately

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Control catastrophic haemorrhage with tourniquet and direct pressure as required
- Record vital signs and monitor closely for haemorrhagic shock
- Provide reassurance, warmth, and positioning for comfort where possible
- Gently dress the stump with sterile dressings
- Retrieve the amputated part, wrap it in sterile moist gauze, place it in a sealed bag, then place that bag in a second container with a cool substance
- Expedite transport and early consultation for retrieval or surgical destination planning

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Establish IV or IO access
- Consider IV fluids for shock or major blood loss
- Provide IV analgesia and antiemetic therapy within scope
- Consider antibiotics and tetanus advice in line with local protocol and senior direction
- Escalate early if crush injury, prolonged extrication, or significant contamination is present
